sourceQuestion:
Consider the following well-formed formulae:
I.   ~forall x (P(x))
II.  ~exists x (P(x))
III. ~exists x (~P(x))
IV.  exists x (~P(x))

Which of the following pairs are logically equivalent?

A. I and III
B. I and IV
C. II and III
D. II and IV
sourceAnswer: B. I and IV

parsed.stem:
Consider the following well-formed formulae:
I.   ~forall x (P(x))
II.  ~exists x (P(x))
III. ~exists x (~P(x))
IV.  exists x (~P(x))

Which of the following pairs are logically equivalent?

options.text: I and III
I and IV
II and III
II and IV

sourceQuestion:
Suppose the predicate F(x, y, t) is used to represent the statement that person x can fool person y at time t. Which one of the statements below expresses best the meaning of the formula,
~(forall x)(exists y)(forall t) F(x, y, t)

A. Everyone can fool some person at some time
B. No one can fool everyone all the time
C. Everyone cannot fool some person all the time
D. No one can fool some person at some time
sourceAnswer: B. No one can fool everyone all the time

parsed.stem:
Suppose the predicate F(x, y, t) is used to represent the statement that person x can fool person y at time t. Which one of the statements below expresses best the meaning of the formula,
~(forall x)(exists y)(forall t) F(x, y, t)

options.text: Everyone can fool some person at some time
No one can fool everyone all the time
Everyone cannot fool some person all the time
No one can fool some person at some time

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following options is CORRECT given three positive integers x, y, and z, and a predicate
P(x) = ~(x = 1) ^ (forall y)(forall z)((x = y * z) -> (y = 1 v z = 1))

A. P(x) being true means that x is a prime number
B. P(x) being true means that x is a number other than 1
C. P(x) is always true irrespective of the value of x
D. P(x) being true means that x has exactly two factors other than 1 and x
sourceAnswer: A. P(x) being true means that x is a prime number

parsed.stem:
Which one of the following options is CORRECT given three positive integers x, y, and z, and a predicate
P(x) = ~(x = 1) ^ (forall y)(forall z)((x = y * z) -> (y = 1 v z = 1))

options.text: P(x) being true means that x is a prime number
P(x) being true means that x is a number other than 1
P(x) is always true irrespective of the value of x
P(x) being true means that x has exactly two factors other than 1 and x

sourceQuestion:
What is the correct translation of the following statement into mathematical logic?
"Some real numbers are rational"

A. (forall x)(real(x) -> rational(x))
B. (forall x)(real(x) ^ rational(x))
C. (exists x)(real(x) ^ rational(x))
D. (exists x)(real(x) -> rational(x))
sourceAnswer: C. (exists x)(real(x) ^ rational(x))

parsed.stem:
What is the correct translation of the following statement into mathematical logic?
"Some real numbers are rational"

options.text: (forall x)(real(x) -> rational(x))
(forall x)(real(x) ^ rational(x))
(exists x)(real(x) ^ rational(x))
(exists x)(real(x) -> rational(x))

sourceQuestion:
What is the logical translation of the following statement?
"None of my friends are perfect."

A. (exists x)(F(x) ^ ~P(x))
B. (exists x)(F(x) -> ~P(x))
C. ~(exists x)(F(x) ^ P(x))
D. (forall x)(F(x) -> ~P(x))
sourceAnswer: D. (forall x)(F(x) -> ~P(x))

parsed.stem:
What is the logical translation of the following statement?
"None of my friends are perfect."

options.text: (exists x)(F(x) ^ ~P(x))
(exists x)(F(x) -> ~P(x))
~(exists x)(F(x) ^ P(x))
(forall x)(F(x) -> ~P(x))

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following is NOT logically equivalent to ~exists x (forall y(p(y)) ^ forall z(q(z))) ?

A. forall x(forall y(~p(y)) v exists z(~q(z)))
B. forall x(forall y(~p(y)) v forall z(~q(z)))
C. ~exists x(exists y(p(y)) ^ exists z(q(z)))
D. forall x(exists y(~p(y)) v exists z(~q(z)))
sourceAnswer: B. forall x(forall y(~p(y)) v forall z(~q(z)))

parsed.stem: Which one of the following is NOT logically equivalent to ~exists x (forall y(p(y)) ^ forall z(q(z))) ?

options.text: forall x(forall y(~p(y)) v exists z(~q(z)))
forall x(forall y(~p(y)) v forall z(~q(z)))
~exists x(exists y(p(y)) ^ exists z(q(z)))
forall x(exists y(~p(y)) v exists z(~q(z)))

sourceQuestion:
Consider the statement "Not all that glitters is gold". Predicate glitters(x) is true if x glitters and predicate gold(x) is true if x is gold. Which one of the following logical formulae represents the above statement?

A. forall x (glitters(x) -> gold(x))
B. exists x (glitters(x) ^ ~gold(x))
C. exists x (glitters(x) -> gold(x))
D. forall x (glitters(x) ^ ~gold(x))
sourceAnswer: D. forall x (glitters(x) ^ ~gold(x))

parsed.stem: Consider the statement "Not all that glitters is gold". Predicate glitters(x) is true if x glitters and predicate gold(x) is true if x is gold. Which one of the following logical formulae represents the above statement?

options.text: forall x (glitters(x) -> gold(x))
exists x (glitters(x) ^ ~gold(x))
exists x (glitters(x) -> gold(x))
forall x (glitters(x) ^ ~gold(x))

sourceQuestion:
The CORRECT formula for the sentence "not all Rainy days are Cold" is:

A. forall d (~Rainy(d) -> Cold(d))
B. exists d (Rainy(d) ^ Cold(d))
C. ~forall d (Rainy(d) ^ Cold(d))
D. exists d (Rainy(d) ^ ~Cold(d))
sourceAnswer: D. exists d (Rainy(d) ^ ~Cold(d))

parsed.stem: The CORRECT formula for the sentence "not all Rainy days are Cold" is:

options.text: forall d (~Rainy(d) -> Cold(d))
exists d (Rainy(d) ^ Cold(d))
~forall d (Rainy(d) ^ Cold(d))
exists d (Rainy(d) ^ ~Cold(d))

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following well-formed formulae is a tautology?

A. forall x exists y R(x,y) -> exists y forall x R(x,y)
B. exists y forall x R(x,y) -> forall x exists y R(x,y)
C. exists y forall x R(x,y) -> forall y exists x R(x,y)
D. forall x exists y R(x,y) -> forall y exists x R(x,y)
sourceAnswer: C. exists y forall x R(x,y) -> forall y exists x R(x,y)

parsed.stem: Which one of the following well-formed formulae is a tautology?

options.text: forall x exists y R(x,y) -> exists y forall x R(x,y)
exists y forall x R(x,y) -> forall x exists y R(x,y)
exists y forall x R(x,y) -> forall y exists x R(x,y)
forall x exists y R(x,y) -> forall y exists x R(x,y)

sourceQuestion:
Which one of the following well-formed formulae in predicate calculus is NOT valid?

A. (forall x p(x) v forall x q(x)) -> forall x (p(x) v q(x))
B. exists x (p(x) ^ q(x)) -> (exists x p(x) ^ exists x q(x))
C. (exists x p(x) v exists x q(x)) -> exists x (p(x) v q(x))
D. forall x (p(x) v q(x)) -> (forall x p(x) v forall x q(x))
sourceAnswer: D. forall x (p(x) v q(x)) -> (forall x p(x) v forall x q(x))

parsed.stem: Which one of the following well-formed formulae in predicate calculus is NOT valid?

options.text: (forall x p(x) v forall x q(x)) -> forall x (p(x) v q(x))
exists x (p(x) ^ q(x)) -> (exists x p(x) ^ exists x q(x))
(exists x p(x) v exists x q(x)) -> exists x (p(x) v q(x))
forall x (p(x) v q(x)) -> (forall x p(x) v forall x q(x))

sourceQuestion:
Consider the first-order logic sentence F = forall x (exists y R(x,y)). Assuming non-empty logical domains, which of the following sentences are implied by F?

I. exists y (forall x R(x,y))
II. exists x (forall y R(x,y))
III. ~forall y (exists x R(x,y))
IV. ~exists y (exists x R(x,y))

A. I and IV
B. I and III
C. III and IV
D. II and III
sourceAnswer: B. I and III

parsed.stem:
Consider the first-order logic sentence F = forall x (exists y R(x,y)). Assuming non-empty logical domains, which of the following sentences are implied by F?

I. exists y (forall x R(x,y))
II. exists x (forall y R(x,y))
III. ~forall y (exists x R(x,y))
IV. ~exists y (exists x R(x,y))

options.text: I and IV
I and III
III and IV
II and III

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following predicate logic formulae/formula is/are CORRECT representation(s) of the statement: "Everyone has exactly one mother"?

The meanings of the predicates used are:
- mother(x, y): x is the mother of y
- noteq(x, y): x and y are not equal

A. forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (mother(z,y) -> z=x)]
B. forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (noteq(z,x) -> ~mother(z,y))]
C. forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (mother(z,y) -> noteq(z,x))]
D. forall y [exists x mother(x,y) ^ forall x forall z ((mother(x,y) ^ mother(z,y)) -> x=z)]
sourceAnswer: B, D

parsed.stem:
Which of the following predicate logic formulae/formula is/are CORRECT representation(s) of the statement: "Everyone has exactly one mother"?

The meanings of the predicates used are:
- mother(x, y): x is the mother of y
- noteq(x, y): x and y are not equal

options.text: forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (mother(z,y) -> z=x)]
forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (noteq(z,x) -> ~mother(z,y))]
forall y exists x [mother(x,y) ^ forall z (mother(z,y) -> noteq(z,x))]
forall y [exists x mother(x,y) ^ forall x forall z ((mother(x,y) ^ mother(z,y)) -> x=z)]

sourceQuestion:
Let P(x) be an arbitrary predicate over the domain of natural numbers. Which ONE of the following statements is TRUE?

A. (P(0) ^ forall x (P(x) -> P(x+1))) => forall y P(y)
B. (P(0) ^ forall x (P(x) -> P(x+1))) => P(1000) ^ (P(1000) => P(0))
C. (P(1000) ^ forall x (P(x+1) -> P(x))) => P(0)
D. (P(1000) ^ forall x (P(x+1) -> P(x))) => forall y P(y)
sourceAnswer: A. (P(0) ^ forall x (P(x) -> P(x+1))) => forall y P(y)

parsed.stem: Let P(x) be an arbitrary predicate over the domain of natural numbers. Which ONE of the following statements is TRUE?

options.text: (P(0) ^ forall x (P(x) -> P(x+1))) => forall y P(y)
(P(1000) ^ forall x (P(x+1) -> P(x))) => forall y P(y)

sourceQuestion:
Let p, q, r be three statements with variables x and y chosen from some universe. Consider the following statement:

(forall x)(exists y)(p -> q) -> (forall x)(exists y)(p -> r)

Which one of the following is its equivalent?

A. (forall x)(exists y)(p -> (q -> r))
B. (forall x)(exists y)p -> (forall x)(exists y)(q -> r)
C. (exists x)(forall y)(~p v r) v (exists x)(forall y)(~p v ~q)
D. (forall x)(exists y)(~p v q) -> (forall x)(exists y)(~p v r)
sourceAnswer: C. (exists x)(forall y)(~p v r) v (exists x)(forall y)(~p v ~q)

parsed.stem:
Let p, q, r be three statements with variables x and y chosen from some universe. Consider the following statement:

(forall x)(exists y)(p -> q) -> (forall x)(exists y)(p -> r)

Which one of the following is its equivalent?

options.text: (forall x)(exists y)(p -> (q -> r))
(forall x)(exists y)p -> (forall x)(exists y)(q -> r)
(exists x)(forall y)(~p v r) v (exists x)(forall y)(~p v ~q)
(forall x)(exists y)(~p v q) -> (forall x)(exists y)(~p v r)

sourceQuestion:
Let P(x) and Q(x) be arbitrary predicates. Which of the following statements is always TRUE?

A. (forall x (P(x) v Q(x))) => ((forall x P(x)) v (forall x Q(x)))
B. (forall x)(P(x) => Q(x)) => ((forall x P(x)) => (forall x Q(x)))
C. (forall x P(x)) ^ (forall x Q(x)) => (forall x)(P(x) v Q(x))
D. (forall x P(x)) v (forall x Q(x)) => (forall x)(P(x) ^ Q(x))
sourceAnswer: B. (forall x)(P(x) => Q(x)) => ((forall x P(x)) => (forall x Q(x)))

parsed.stem: Let P(x) and Q(x) be arbitrary predicates. Which of the following statements is always TRUE?

options.text: (forall x (P(x) v Q(x))) => ((forall x P(x)) v (forall x Q(x)))
(forall x)(P(x) => Q(x)) => ((forall x P(x)) => (forall x Q(x)))
(forall x P(x)) ^ (forall x Q(x)) => (forall x)(P(x) v Q(x))
(forall x P(x)) v (forall x Q(x)) => (forall x)(P(x) ^ Q(x))

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following first order logic formula in which R is a binary relation symbol.

forall y (R(y, x) => R(g, y))

The formula is

A. satisfiable and valid
B. satisfiable and so is its negation
C. unsatisfiable but its negation is valid
D. satisfiable but its negation is unsatisfiable
sourceAnswer: B. satisfiable and so is its negation

parsed.stem:
Consider the following first order logic formula in which R is a binary relation symbol.

forall y (R(y, x) => R(g, y))

The formula is

options.text: satisfiable and valid
satisfiable and so is its negation
unsatisfiable but its negation is valid
satisfiable but its negation is unsatisfiable

sourceQuestion:
Which one of these first-order logic formulae is valid?

A. forall x P(x) => forall x Q(x) => forall x (P(x) => Q(x))
B. exists x P(x) ^ exists x Q(x) => exists x (P(x) ^ Q(x))
C. forall x (P(x) v Q(x)) => (forall x P(x)) v (forall x Q(x))
D. forall x P(x) v Q(x) => forall x (P(x) v Q(x))
sourceAnswer: A. forall x P(x) => forall x Q(x) => forall x (P(x) => Q(x))

parsed.stem: Which one of these first-order logic formulae is valid?

options.text: forall x P(x) => forall x Q(x) => forall x (P(x) => Q(x))
exists x P(x) ^ exists x Q(x) => exists x (P(x) ^ Q(x))
forall x (P(x) v Q(x)) => (forall x P(x)) v (forall x Q(x))
forall x P(x) v Q(x) => forall x (P(x) v Q(x))

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following first order formulae is logically valid? Here alpha(x) is a first order formula with x as a free variable, and beta is a first order formula with no free variable.

A. (forall x alpha(x)) => (exists x alpha(x))
B. (exists x alpha(x)) => (forall x alpha(x))
C. alpha(x) => (exists x alpha(x))
D. (forall x (alpha(x) => beta)) => ((forall x alpha(x)) => beta)
sourceAnswer: C. alpha(x) => (exists x alpha(x))

parsed.stem: Which of the following first order formulae is logically valid? Here alpha(x) is a first order formula with x as a free variable, and beta is a first order formula with no free variable.

options.text: (forall x alpha(x)) => (exists x alpha(x))
(exists x alpha(x)) => (forall x alpha(x))
alpha(x) => (exists x alpha(x))
(forall x (alpha(x) => beta)) => ((forall x alpha(x)) => beta)

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is the negation of [forall x, exists y, (alpha(y, z_n) v ~beta(y, z_n))]?

A. forall x, exists y, (~alpha(y, z_n) ^ beta(y, z_n))
B. exists x, exists y, (~alpha(y, z_n) ^ beta(y, z_n))
C. forall x, forall y, (~alpha(y, z_n) v beta(y, z_n))
D. exists x, forall y, (~alpha(y, z_n) ^ beta(y, z_n))
sourceAnswer: D. exists x, forall y, (~alpha(y, z_n) ^ beta(y, z_n))

parsed.stem: Which of the following is the negation of [forall x, exists y, (alpha(y, z_n) v ~beta(y, z_n))]?

options.text: forall x, exists y, (~alpha(y, z_n) ^ beta(y, z_n))
exists x, exists y, (~alpha(y, z_n) ^ beta(y, z_n))
forall x, forall y, (~alpha(y, z_n) v beta(y, z_n))
exists x, forall y, (~alpha(y, z_n) ^ beta(y, z_n))

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following logical inferences.

I_1: If it rains then the cricket match will not be played. The cricket match was played. Inference: There was no rain.

I_2: If it rains then the cricket match will not be played. It did not rain. Inference: The cricket match was played.

Which of the following is TRUE?

A. Both I_1 and I_2 are correct inferences
B. I_1 is correct but I_2 is not a correct inference
C. I_1 is not correct but I_2 is a correct inference
D. Both I_1 and I_2 are not correct inferences
sourceAnswer: B. I_1 is correct but I_2 is not a correct inference

parsed.stem:
Consider the following logical inferences.

I_1: If it rains then the cricket match will not be played. The cricket match was played. Inference: There was no rain.

I_2: If it rains then the cricket match will not be played. It did not rain. Inference: The cricket match was played.

Which of the following is TRUE?

options.text: Both I_1 and I_2 are correct inferences
I_1 is correct but I_2 is not a correct inference
I_1 is not correct but I_2 is a correct inference
Both I_1 and I_2 are not correct inferences

sourceQuestion:
Consider the following two statements.

S_1: If a candidate is known to be corrupt, then he will not be elected.
S_2: If a candidate is kind, he will be elected.

Which one of the following statements follows from S_1 and S_2 as per sound inference rules of logic?

A. If a person is known to be corrupt, he is kind
B. If a person is not known to be corrupt, he is kind
C. If a person is kind, he is not known to be corrupt
D. If a person is not kind, he is not known to be corrupt
sourceAnswer: C. If a person is kind, he is not known to be corrupt

parsed.stem:
Consider the following two statements.

S_1: If a candidate is known to be corrupt, then he will not be elected.
S_2: If a candidate is kind, he will be elected.

Which one of the following statements follows from S_1 and S_2 as per sound inference rules of logic?

options.text: If a person is known to be corrupt, he is kind
If a person is not known to be corrupt, he is kind
If a person is kind, he is not known to be corrupt
If a person is not kind, he is not known to be corrupt

sourceQuestion:
In a room there are only two types of people, namely Type 1 and Type 2. Type 1 people always tell the truth and Type 2 people always lie. You give a fair coin to a person in that room, without knowing which type he is from, and tell him to toss it and hide the result from you till you ask for it. Upon asking the person replies the following:

"The result of the toss is head if and only if I am of Type 2."

Which of the following conclusions is correct?

A. The result is head
B. The result is tail
C. If the result is Type 2 then the result is tail
D. If the result is Type 1, then the result is tail
sourceAnswer: A. The result is head

parsed.stem:
In a room there are only two types of people, namely Type 1 and Type 2. Type 1 people always tell the truth and Type 2 people always lie. You give a fair coin to a person in that room, without knowing which type he is from, and tell him to toss it and hide the result from you till you ask for it. Upon asking the person replies the following:

"The result of the toss is head if and only if I am of Type 2."

Which of the following conclusions is correct?

options.text: The result is head
The result is tail
If the result is Type 2 then the result is tail
If the result is Type 1, then the result is tail

sourceQuestion:
The proposition p ^ (~p v q) is:
A. a tautology
B. logically equivalent to p ^ q
C. logically equivalent to p ^ ~q
D. a contradiction
E. none of the above
sourceAnswer: B. logically equivalent to p ^ q

parsed.stem: The proposition p ^ (~p v q) is:

options.text: a tautology
logically equivalent to p ^ q
logically equivalent to p ^ ~q
a contradiction
none of the above

sourceQuestion:
If the proposition p => q is true, then the truth value of the proposition ~p v (p => ~q), where ~ is negation, v is inclusive OR and => is implication, is

A. True
B. Multiple Values
C. False
D. Cannot be determined
sourceAnswer: D. Cannot be determined

parsed.stem: If the proposition p => q is true, then the truth value of the proposition ~p v (p => ~q), where ~ is negation, v is inclusive OR and => is implication, is

options.text: True
Multiple Values
False
Cannot be determined

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following is NOT True? (Read ^ as AND, v as OR, ~ as NOT, -> as one way implication and <-> as two way implication)

A. ((x -> y) ^ z) -> y
B. ((x -> y) ^ (~x -> y)) -> y
C. (x v y) -> (~x -> y)
D. ((x v y) <-> (~x -> ~y))

parsed.stem: Which of the following is NOT True? (Read ^ as AND, v as OR, ~ as NOT, -> as one way implication and <-> as two way implication)

sourceQuestion:
Which of the following propositions is a tautology?

A. (p -> q) -> p
B. p -> (q -> p)
C. p -> (p -> q)
D. p -> (p /\ q)

parsed.stem: Which of the following propositions is a tautology?

sourceQuestion:
What is the converse of the following assertion?

'I stay only if you go'

A. I stay if you go
B. If I stay then you go
C. If you do not go then I do not stay
D. If I do not stay then you go
sourceAnswer: A. I stay if you go

parsed.stem:
What is the converse of the following assertion?

'I stay only if you go'

options.text: I stay if you go
If I stay then you go
If you do not go then I do not stay
If I do not stay then you go

sourceQuestion:
Let a, b, c, d be propositions. Assume that the equivalence a <-> (~b /\ c) and b <-> c hold. Then the truth-value of the formula (a /\ b) \/ (~a /\ ~b) is always:

A. True
B. False
C. Same as the truth-value of b
D. Same as the truth-value of d
sourceAnswer: A. True

parsed.stem: Let a, b, c, d be propositions. Assume that the equivalence a <-> (~b /\ c) and b <-> c hold. Then the truth-value of the formula (a /\ b) \/ (~a /\ ~b) is always:

options.text: True
False
Same as the truth-value of b
Same as the truth-value of d

sourceQuestion:
Consider two well-formed formulas in propositional logic:
F_1 : P -> ~P
F_2 : (P -> ~P) /\ (~P -> P)

Which one of the following statements is correct?

A. F_1 is satisfiable, F_2 is valid
B. F_1 is unsatisfiable, F_2 is satisfiable
C. F_1 is unsatisfiable, F_2 is valid
D. F_1 and F_2 are both satisfiable
sourceAnswer: A. F_1 is satisfiable, F_2 is valid

parsed.stem:
Consider two well-formed formulas in propositional logic:
F_1 : P -> ~P
F_2 : (P -> ~P) /\ (~P -> P)

Which one of the following statements is correct?

options.text: F_1 is satisfiable, F_2 is valid
F_1 is unsatisfiable, F_2 is satisfiable
F_1 is unsatisfiable, F_2 is valid
F_1 and F_2 are both satisfiable

sourceQuestion:
'If X then Y unless Z' is represented by which of the following formulas in propositional logic? ('-' is negation, '/\' is conjunction, '->' is implication)

A. (X /\ ~Z) -> Y
B. (X -> Y) /\ (~Z)
C. X -> (Y /\ ~Z)
D. (X /\ ~Z) -> ~Y

parsed.stem: 'If X then Y unless Z' is represented by which of the following formulas in propositional logic? ('-' is negation, '/\' is conjunction, '->' is implication)
